name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
إِذَا وَقَعَتِ ٱلْوَاقِعَةُ (١)
```

```english
When the Imminent [Hour] befalls (1)
```

```arabic
لَيْسَ لِوَقْعَتِهَا كَاذِبَةٌ (٢)
```

```english
—there is no denying that it will befall— (2)
```

```arabic
خَافِضَةٌ رَّافِعَةٌ (٣)
```

```english
[it will be] lowering and exalting. (3)
```

```arabic
إِذَا رُجَّتِ ٱلْأَرْضُ رَجًّا (٤)
```

```english
When the earth is shaken violently, (4)
```

```arabic
وَبُسَّتِ ٱلْجِبَالُ بَسًّا (٥)
```

```english
and the mountains are shattered into bits (5)
```

```arabic
فَكَانَتْ هَبَآءً مُّنۢبَثًّا (٦)
```

```english
and become scattered dust, (6)
```

```arabic
وَكُنتُمْ أَزْوَٰجًا ثَلَـٰثَةً (٧)
```

```english
you will be three groups: (7)
```

```arabic
فَأَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْمَيْمَنَةِ مَآ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْمَيْمَنَةِ (٨)
```

```english
The People of the Right Hand—and what are the People of the Right Hand?! (8)
```

```arabic
وَأَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْمَشْـَٔمَةِ مَآ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْمَشْـَٔمَةِ (٩)
```

```english
And the People of the Left Hand—and what are the People of the Left Hand?! (9)
```

```arabic
وَٱلسَّـٰبِقُونَ ٱلسَّـٰبِقُونَ (١٠)
```

```english
And the Foremost Ones are the foremost ones: (10)
```

## (Commentary)

The Surah describes the Great Resurrection (القيامة الكبرى) — *the final and universal day of rising when all humanity is brought back to life for judgment* — in which the people are resurrected, held to account, and recompensed. It first mentions some of its terrors that closely affect man and the earth he inhabits, noting the overturning of conditions and states through abasement and elevation, the violent shaking of the earth, and the crumbling of the mountains. It categorizes people into three groups (أزواج) — *distinct classes or types of beings* — in general terms, and then details the final state of each: the Foremost (السابقون), the People of the Right (أصحاب اليمين), and the People of the Left (أصحاب الشمال).

Thereafter, it presents arguments against the People of the Left who deny His Lordship (الربوبية) and the Resurrection (البعث), and who reject the Quran that calls toward monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — and faith in the Resurrection. The Surah concludes by mentioning the moment of death (الاحتضار) — *the state of the soul as it prepares to leave the body* — with the descent of death and the division of people into these three groups.

The Surah is Meccan, as evidenced by the context of its verses.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِذا وَقَعَتِ الْواقِعَةُ** **"When the Event befalls" (1).** The "befalling" (وقوع) of an incident is its occurrence. "The Event" (الواقعة) is an attribute used to describe any incident; however, what is intended here is the Event of the Resurrection. It has been used as a proper noun (إطلاق الأعلام), as if it requires no estimated noun to qualify, which is why it is said to be among the names of the Resurrection in the Quran, like "the Inevitable" (الحاقة), "the Rattler" (القارعة), and "the Overwhelmer" (الغاشية).

The phrase **إِذا وَقَعَتِ الْواقِعَةُ** carries the meaning of a conditional clause, yet the apodosis (جزاء الشرط) — *the result or consequence of the condition* — is omitted to magnify its gravity and emphasize its significance. In any case, it is understood from what the Surah will describe regarding the state of people on the Day of Resurrection; the estimation would be something like: "the believers shall triumph and the disbelievers shall lose."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لَيْسَ لِوَقْعَتِها كاذِبَةٌ** **"there is no denying its happening" (2).** It is stated in *al-Majma‘* that "denying/lie" (الكاذبة) is a verbal noun (مصدر) like "well-being" (العافية) and "outcome" (العاقبة). Accordingly, the meaning is: there is no falsehood (كذب) in its occurrence or its realization. It is also said that "denying" (كاذبة) is an adjective whose qualified noun is omitted, the estimation being: "there is no false soul/claim (قضية كاذبة) regarding its occurrence."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **خافِضَةٌ رافِعَةٌ** **"abasing [some], exalting [others]" (3).** These are two predicates whose subject is the pronoun referring back to "the Event." Abasing (الخفض) is the opposite of exalting (الرفع). Its being "abasing and exalting" is an allusion (كناية) to its overturning of the observed system of the world; thus, the inner secrets (السرائر) will manifest while they are hidden today, and the effects and links of secondary causes (الأسباب) will be veiled and concealed while they are apparent today. It shall humiliate the "mighty" among the folk of disbelief and wickedness, and exalt the God-conscious (المتقين).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِذا رُجَّتِ الْأَرْضُ رَجًّا** **"when the earth is shaken with a violent shaking" (4).** Shaking (الرج) is to move something with great intensity, referring to the earthquake of the Hour (زلزلة الساعة) which Allah, the Glorified, magnifies in His saying: **إِنَّ زَلْزَلَةَ السَّاعَةِ شَيْءٌ عَظِيمٌ** **"Indeed the quaking of the Hour is a terrible thing" (22:1).** He has magnified it in this verse by expressing it as the "shaking of the earth," then emphasized its intensity by the indefiniteness of His saying **رَجًّا** (a shaking), meaning a shaking whose intensity cannot be described. This sentence serves as a substitute (بدل) or an explanation (بيان) for His saying: **إِذا وَقَعَتِ الْواقِعَةُ**.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَبُسَّتِ الْجِبالُ بَسًّا فَكانَت هَباءً مُنْبَثًّا** **"and the mountains are crumbled into powder, and become scattered dust" (5-6).** This is coordinated (عطف) with "shaken" (رُجَّت). Crumbling (البس) is to crush (الفت); it is the reduction of a body into tiny, vanishing parts like flour through pounding or the like. It is also said that *al-bass* means to set in motion, thus carrying the meaning of His saying: **وَسُيِّرَتِ الْجِبالُ** **"and the mountains will be set in motion" (78:20).**

His saying: **فَكانَت هَباءً مُنْبَثًّا** — Dust (الهباء) is said to be fine dust (الغبار), or the motes of dust visible in a ray of sunlight entering through an aperture. Scattering (الانبثاث) is dispersion (التفرق). The meaning is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَكُنْتُمْ أَزْواجاً ثَلاثَةً** **"and you are three groups" (7).** Group (الزوج) here means category or class (الصنف), and the address is to all of humanity.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَأَصْحابُ الْمَيْمَنَةِ ما أَصْحابُ الْمَيْمَنَةِ** **"As for the People of the Right Hand, who are the People of the Right Hand?" (8).** This branches off from what preceded it as an explanation (بيان) branches from the thing explained (المبين). This verse and the two following it are an explanation of the three groups.

The "Right Hand" (الميمنة) is derived from "auspiciousness" (اليمن), which is the opposite of "ill-fortune" (الشؤم). Thus, the People of the Right Hand are the people of felicity (السعادة) and auspiciousness, in contrast to the People of the Left Hand (أصحاب المشأمة) — the people of wretchedness (الشقاء) and ill-fortune. The view that "Right Hand" (الميمنة) refers to the right side (اليمين) because they are given their records in their right hands while others are given them in their left is refuted by the juxtaposition of the People of the Right Hand with the People of the Left Hand (أصحاب المشأمة). Had it been as suggested, it would have said "the People of the Left" (أصحاب الشمال), as is apparent.

The word "what" (ما) in His saying **ما أَصْحابُ الْمَيْمَنَةِ** is interrogative (استفهامية) and serves as a subject (مبتدأ) whose predicate (خبر) is **أَصْحابُ الْمَيْمَنَةِ**. The entire phrase then serves as the predicate for the initial **فَأَصْحابُ الْمَيْمَنَةِ**. The interrogation serves to magnify their matter and exalt their status.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَأَصْحابُ الْمَشْئَمَةِ ما أَصْحابُ الْمَشْئَمَةِ** **"And the People of the Left Hand, who are the People of the Left Hand?" (9).** The "Left Hand" (المشأمة) is a verbal noun like "ill-fortune" (الشؤم), the opposite of the right. Thus, the "Right Hand" (الميمنة) and "Left Hand" (المشأمة) signify felicity and wretchedness.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَالسَّابِقُونَ السَّابِقُونَ** **"And the Foremost Ones are the Foremost Ones" (10).** That which is suitable to interpret the first "Foremost" (السابقون) is His saying, the Exalted: **فَمِنْهُمْ ظالِمٌ لِنَفْسِهِ وَمِنْهُمْ مُقْتَصِدٌ وَمِنْهُمْ سابِقٌ بِالْخَيْراتِ بِإِذْنِ اللهِ** **"Then some of them are wrongdoers to themselves, and some of them are average, and some of them are foremost in good deeds by Allah’s will" (35:32)**; and His saying: **وَلِكُلٍّ وِجْهَةٌ هُوَ مُوَلِّيها فَاسْتَبِقُوا الْخَيْراتِ** **"Everyone has a direction to which he turns; so hasten to take the lead in all good works" (2:148)**; and His saying: **أُولئِكَ يُسارِعُونَ فِي الْخَيْراتِ وَهُمْ لَها سابِقُونَ** **"it is they who hasten to good deeds, and they are the foremost in them" (23:61).**

Thus, what is intended by the first "Foremost" in the verse are those who are foremost in righteous deeds (الخيرات). When they are foremost in good deeds, they are foremost in reaching the forgiveness and mercy that correspond to them, as He, the Exalted, said: **سابِقُوا إِلى مَغْفِرَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ وَجَنَّةٍ** **"Take the lead towards forgiveness from your Lord and at Paradise" (57:21).** So, those foremost in good deeds are those foremost in mercy, which is His saying: **وَالسَّابِقُونَ السَّابِقُونَ**.

It is also said that the second "Foremost" is the same as the first, similar to the poetic expression: "I am Abu al-Najm, and my poetry is my poetry."

In the phrase **وَالسَّابِقُونَ السَّابِقُونَ**, the first is the subject and the second is the predicate. It is also said that the first is the subject and the second is an emphasis (تأكيد), while the predicate is His saying: **أُولئِكَ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ** **"They are the ones brought near" (11).**

There are other opinions regarding the interpretation of "the Foremost": it is said they are those who hasten to everything Allah has called toward; it is said they are those who preceded in faith and obedience without hesitation; it is said they are the Prophets (عليه السلام) because they are the leaders of the followers of religions; it is said they are the Believer of the People of Pharaoh, Habib al-Najjar mentioned in Surah Ya-Sin, and Ali (عليه السلام) who was the first to believe in the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and is the most excellent of them; it is said they are those who were foremost in the Migration (الهجرة); it is said they are those who are foremost in the five daily prayers; it is said they are those who prayed toward both Qiblahs; it is said they are those foremost in Jihad; and other things have been said.

The first two opinions return to the meaning previously mentioned. The third and fourth should be understood as examples (التمثيل), and the rest are as you see, unless they are also taken as types of examples.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Khisal*, narrated from al-Zuhri, who said: I heard Ali ibn al-Husayn (عليه السلام) saying: "Whoever does not find solace in the solace of Allah, his soul will be torn apart by regrets over the world. By Allah, the world and the Hereafter are but like the two pans of a scale—whichever one outweighs the other takes the other away." Then he recited the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **إِذا وَقَعَتِ الْواقِعَةُ** — meaning the Resurrection — **لَيْسَ لِوَقْعَتِها كاذِبَةٌ خافِضَةٌ** — "By Allah, it abases the enemies of Allah into the Fire," **رافِعَةٌ** — "By Allah, it exalts the friends of Allah to Paradise."

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*: Regarding **إِذا وَقَعَتِ الْواقِعَةُ لَيْسَ لِوَقْعَتِها كاذِبَةٌ**, he said: "The Resurrection is a truth." Regarding His saying **خافِضَةٌ**, he said: "With the enemies of Allah," **رافِعَةٌ** — "For the friends of Allah." Regarding **إِذا رُجَّتِ الْأَرْضُ رَجًّا**, he said: "Parts of it will crash against others." Regarding **وَبُسَّتِ الْجِبالُ بَسًّا**, he said: "The mountains will be uprooted entirely." Regarding **فَكانَت هَباءً مُنْبَثًّا**, he said: "The dust (الهباء) is that which is in the aperture from the sun's rays."

Regarding His saying **وَكُنْتُمْ أَزْواجاً ثَلاثَةً**, he said: "On the Day of Resurrection," **فَأَصْحابُ الْمَيْمَنَةِ ما أَصْحابُ الْمَيْمَنَةِ وَأَصْحابُ الْمَشْئَمَةِ ما أَصْحابُ الْمَشْئَمَةِ وَالسَّابِقُونَ السَّابِقُونَ** — "Those who were foremost to Paradise."

**I say:** His saying "those who were foremost to Paradise" is an interpretation of the second "Foremost."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn Jarir, and Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from Ali ibn Abi Talib (عليه السلام) that he said: "The scattered dust (الهباء المنبث) is the stirred-up motes (رهج الذرات), and the dispersed dust (الهباء المنثور) is the sun-dust that you see in the rays from an aperture."

In the same source, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas regarding His saying **وَالسَّابِقُونَ السَّابِقُونَ**: "It was revealed concerning Ezekiel, the Believer of the People of Pharaoh; Habib al-Najjar who is mentioned in Ya-Sin; and Ali ibn Abi Talib. Each of these men was the foremost of his nation, and Ali is the most excellent of them in precedence."

In *al-Majma‘*, narrated from Abu Ja‘far (عليه السلام), who said: "The Foremost are four: the son of Adam who was killed; the foremost of the nation of Moses, who was the Believer of the People of Pharaoh; the foremost of the nation of Jesus, who was Habib; and the foremost in the nation of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), who is Ali ibn Abi Talib (عليه السلام)."

**I say:** This meaning is also narrated in *Rawdat al-Wa‘izin* from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام).

In *Amali al-Shaykh*, with its chain to Ibn Abbas, who said: I asked the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **وَالسَّابِقُونَ السَّابِقُونَ أُولئِكَ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ فِي جَنَّاتِ النَّعِيمِ**. He said: "Gabriel said to me: That is Ali and his followers (شيعته); they are the foremost to Paradise, brought near to Allah by His honoring of them."

In *Kamal al-Din*, with its chain to Khaythamah al-Ju‘fi from Abu Ja‘far (عليه السلام) in a hadith: "And we are the Foremost (السابقون السابقون) and we are the Last."

In *al-Uyun*, in the chapter on what has been narrated from Imam al-Rida (عليه السلام) of collected reports, with its chain to Ali (عليه السلام), who said: "**وَالسَّابِقُونَ السَّابِقُونَ أُولئِكَ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ** was revealed concerning me."

In *al-Majma‘*, regarding the verse: "It is said: [Foremost] to the five daily prayers." Narrated from Ali (عليه السلام).

**I say:** The correct approach is to treat all these reports as examples (التمثيل), as previously mentioned.
